disclaimer: Seed data compiled from public sources. Every row confirmed_by_counsel=false until ratified by immigration counsel. Not legal advice.
key_conventions: status_or_transition_key 'all' is a sentinel meaning the rule applies to every status in statuses.json (I-9/E-Verify obligations are population-wide, not status-specific). It is not a key in statuses.json or transitions.json; the seed loader/validator must special-case it instead of resolving it as a foreign key.

rules.value: No later than the employee's first day of employment for pay; may be completed any time after acceptance of a job offer, but never before offer acceptance
rules.source_citation: 8 CFR 274a.2(b)(1)(i)(A); USCIS I-9 Central, Completing Section 1
rules.notes: Regulation text says 'at the time of hire'; USCIS operational guidance interprets this as no later than the first day of employment and not before offer acceptance. Longstanding IRCA-era rule; verification obligations began 1987-06-01.

rules.source_citation: 8 CFR 274a.2(b)(1)(ii); USCIS I-9 Central, Completing Section 2
rules.notes: Employer must physically or (if qualified) remotely examine documents and complete/sign Section 2 within 3 business days after the employee's first day of employment for pay. Example per USCIS: start Monday, complete by Thursday.

rules.value: If the individual is hired for a duration of fewer than 3 business days, Section 1 and Section 2 must both be fully completed no later than the first day of employment for pay; receipts may not be accepted
rules.notes: Receipts in lieu of documents are not permitted for hires lasting fewer than 3 business days.

rules.value: One unexpired List A document (identity AND employment authorization) OR one unexpired List B document (identity) plus one unexpired List C document (employment authorization), or an acceptable receipt
rules.source_citation: INA 274A(b)(1); 8 CFR 274a.2(b)(1)(v); USCIS Lists of Acceptable Documents
rules.notes: Current list structure derives from the 1997 interim designation of acceptable documents (62 FR 51001) as later amended. E-Verify employers additionally require a List B document to contain a photograph.

rules.source_citation: 8 U.S.C. 1324b(a)(6) (unfair documentary practices)
rules.notes: Employer may not request more or different documents than required, may not demand specific documents, and may not reject documents that reasonably appear genuine and relate to the individual, when done for a discriminatory purpose. Added by Immigration Act of 1990; intent element added by IIRIRA (1996). Enforced by DOJ Immigrant and Employee Rights Section (IER).

rules.source_citation: 8 CFR 274a.2(b)(1)(v); Documents Acceptable for Employment Eligibility Verification, 73 FR 76505 (Dec. 17, 2008) (interim final rule, effective Apr. 3, 2009)
rules.notes: All List A/B/C documents must be unexpired when presented. Requirement introduced by the Dec. 17, 2008 interim final rule (73 FR 76505); original Feb. 2, 2009 effective date was delayed 60 days to Apr. 3, 2009 (74 FR 5899). Prior seed value gave an incorrect 2016-11-14 effective date. Exceptions operate via receipt rules and via automatic extensions of certain EADs (see ead-auto-extension rows), and expired Permanent Resident Cards are not reverified.

rules.value: A foreign passport with Form I-94/I-94A bearing the same name and an endorsement of the individual's nonimmigrant status and authorization to work for a specific employer qualifies as List A, valid while the endorsement is unexpired and the employment does not conflict with I-94 restrictions
rules.source_citation: 8 CFR 274a.2(b)(1)(v)(A)(5); USCIS Lists of Acceptable Documents, List A item 5
rules.notes: Primary List A path for employer-specific nonimmigrant workers (e.g., h1b_active, l1, o1, tn, e3). A Form I-94 issued to work-authorized nonimmigrants because of status (e.g., asylees) can separately serve as a List C item 7 document.

rules.source_citation: 8 CFR 274a.2(b)(1)(vi)(A); USCIS I-9 Central, Receipts
rules.notes: Receipt for an application to replace a LOST, STOLEN, or DAMAGED List A/B/C document is acceptable; the actual replacement document (or, per USCIS guidance, an alternative acceptable document) must be presented within 90 days of the date of hire or, for reverification, of the date employment authorization expires. Not valid for hires of fewer than 3 business days.

rules.value: Form I-94/I-94A with a temporary I-551 (ADIT) stamp and photograph is a List A receipt valid until the stamp's expiration date, or 1 year after the I-94 issuance date if the stamp has no expiration date; the actual Form I-551 must then be presented
rules.source_citation: 8 CFR 274a.2(b)(1)(vi)(B); USCIS I-9 Central, Receipts; M-274 section 7.1
rules.notes: A machine-readable immigrant visa (MRIV) with 'UPON ENDORSEMENT SERVES AS TEMPORARY I-551 EVIDENCING PERMANENT RESIDENCE FOR 1 YEAR' text in the foreign passport is likewise evidence of LPR status for 1 year from admission.

rules.source_citation: 8 CFR 274a.2(b)(1)(vi)(C); USCIS I-9 Central, Receipts; M-274 section 7.3
rules.notes: Form I-94/I-94A with unexpired refugee admission stamp or computer-generated I-94 printout with admission class 'RE' is a List A receipt for 90 days from hire (or reverification); at the end of the period the employee must present an EAD (Form I-766) or a List B document plus an unrestricted Social Security card.

rules.value: 3 years after the date of hire OR 1 year after the date employment is terminated, whichever is later
rules.notes: Applies per individual Form I-9. For current employees, the form must always be retained.

rules.value: Paper, microfilm, microfiche, or electronic storage meeting the integrity/audit-trail standards of 8 CFR 274a.2(e)-(i)
rules.notes: Electronic retention/signature rules finalized in 2006 (71 FR 34510) as amended in 2010.

rules.value: Copying presented documents is optional and copies must be retained with the Form I-9 or the employee's record; if copies are made, the practice must be applied consistently and not selectively by national origin or citizenship status. Copies are MANDATORY for E-Verify photo-matching documents and for all documents examined under the alternative remote procedure
rules.source_citation: 8 CFR 274a.2(b)(3); E-Verify MOU art. II.A; 88 FR 47990
rules.notes: Copying does not relieve the employer of the obligation to fully complete Section 2.
Employers must be given at least 3 business days' notice before Forms I-9 must be produced for inspection by DHS/ICE, DOL, or IER.

rules.value: Edition date 01/20/2025, OMB expiration 05/31/2027
rules.source_citation: USCIS I-9 Central news, Minor Changes to Form I-9 and E-Verify Updates (Apr. 2, 2025)
rules.notes: Confirmed current as of 2026-07-06; no newer edition found. Changes vs 08/01/23 edition: Section 1 checkbox 4 renamed 'An alien authorized to work', two List B document descriptions say 'sex' instead of 'gender', instructions/DHS Privacy Notice revised.

rules.value: Edition date 08/01/2023; printings showing expiration 05/31/2027 remain usable until that date; printings showing expiration 07/31/2026 are usable only through 2026-07-31
rules.source_citation: USCIS I-9 Central news (Apr. 2, 2025)
rules.notes: As of 2026-07-06 the 07/31/2026-expiring printing is still usable for 25 more days. Beginning 2026-08-01 only forms bearing the 05/31/2027 expiration may be used for new hires.

rules.source_citation: USCIS I-9 Central news (Apr. 2, 2025)
rules.notes: Employers using electronic Form I-9 systems must update to the version bearing the 05/31/2027 expiration date by July 31, 2026.

rules.value: An alien authorized to work (renamed from 'A noncitizen authorized to work' on the 01/20/2025 edition; E-Verify citizenship-status selection updated 2025-04-03)
rules.source_citation: USCIS I-9 Central news (Apr. 2, 2025)
rules.notes: If an employee attests as 'A noncitizen authorized to work' on an older still-valid edition, the employer selects 'An alien authorized to work' in E-Verify.

rules.value: The Spanish-language Form I-9 may be completed for record-keeping only by employers in Puerto Rico; elsewhere it may be used only as a translation aid
rules.source_citation: USCIS Form I-9 page and instructions
rules.notes: Longstanding rule restated in current form instructions.

rules.value: Any person may be designated as the employer's authorized representative to complete and sign Section 2/Supplement B on the employer's behalf; the employer remains fully liable for violations
rules.source_citation: 8 CFR 274a.2(b)(1)(ii); USCIS M-274 section 4.0
rules.notes: Commonly used for remote hires when the employer does not qualify for or use the alternative remote-examination procedure.

rules.value: Only employers enrolled in E-Verify and in good standing ('qualified employers') may use the alternative (remote) document examination procedure, and only at hiring sites enrolled in E-Verify; they must create E-Verify cases for employees verified remotely
rules.notes: DHS Federal Register notice effective Aug. 1, 2023, issued under the framework rule authorizing alternative examination procedures (same-day companion rule). Still in force as of 2026-07-06; no rescission or injunction found. Employers may offer it to remote hires only, but must apply it consistently and non-discriminatorily at a given site.

rules.value: Within the normal 3-business-day Section 2 window: (1) receive and examine copies (front and back if two-sided) of the documents or acceptable receipt; (2) conduct a live video interaction with the individual, who presents the same documents, to assess genuineness and relation to the individual; (3) check the 'alternative procedure' box in Section 2 (or annotate for reverification on Supplement B); (4) retain clear, legible copies of all documents
rules.source_citation: 88 FR 47990 (Jul. 25, 2023); USCIS I-9 Central, Remote Examination of Documents
rules.notes: The 08/01/23 and 01/20/25 form editions include a dedicated checkbox; employers who used the COVID-19 flexibilities (Mar. 20, 2020-Jul. 31, 2023) and were E-Verify participants during that period were permitted to use the alternative procedure to satisfy the Aug. 30, 2023 physical-inspection catch-up requirement.
Clear and legible copies of ALL documents presented (front and back where two-sided) must be retained with the Form I-9 and produced in an audit.

rules.value: Reverification (Form I-9 Supplement B) must be completed no later than the date the employee's current employment authorization or employment-authorization documentation expires
rules.notes: Employee may present any unexpired List A or List C document of their choice (or acceptable receipt); Supplement B replaced the former Section 3 with the 08/01/23 edition. Best practice per USCIS: remind employees at least 90 days before expiration.

rules.source_citation: 8 CFR 274a.2(b)(1)(vii); USCIS M-274
rules.notes: U.S. citizens and noncitizen nationals are never reverified. Reverifying them can constitute an unfair immigration-related employment practice under 8 U.S.C. 1324b.

rules.source_citation: 8 CFR 274a.2(b)(1)(vii); USCIS M-274 section 7.1
rules.notes: Do not reverify an LPR who presented a Permanent Resident Card (Form I-551), even after the card expires. Reverification IS required when the LPR presented a receipt-type document (e.g., I-94 with temporary I-551/ADIT stamp) that expires.

rules.source_citation: 8 CFR 274a.2(b)(1)(vii); USCIS M-274
rules.notes: Expiring List B identity documents are never reverified. Expired U.S. passports and expired Permanent Resident Cards also do not trigger reverification.

rules.value: Supplement B (formerly Section 3) is used for: (1) reverification of expiring employment authorization; (2) rehires within 3 years of the date of the original Form I-9 (in lieu of a new form); (3) recording a legal name change
rules.source_citation: 8 CFR 274a.2(b)(1)(viii); USCIS I-9 Central, Completing Supplement B
rules.notes: Supplement B introduced with the 08/01/23 form edition; retained in the 01/20/25 edition.

rules.value: E-Verify employers must regularly generate the E-Verify Status Change Report to identify employees whose EADs DHS has revoked (e.g., CHNV parole terminations) and must reverify affected current employees using Supplement B within a reasonable time; do NOT create a new E-Verify case and do not accept the revoked EAD; employee may present any other unexpired List A or List C documentation
rules.source_citation: E-Verify, EAD Revocation Guidance for E-Verify Employers (Jun. 20, 2025, updated Jul. 15, 2025)
rules.notes: Status Change Report launched 2025-06-20, replacing Case Status Alerts for EAD revocations; a 'Revoked Document Number' field was added 2025-07-15 so employers can match the specific EAD card number presented at hire. Follows the Supreme Court's May 30, 2025 order allowing termination of CHNV parole.

Permanently increased the automatic extension for timely filed Form I-765 renewals in eligible categories (e.g., c9 adjustment applicants, a12/c19 TPS, a5 asylees, certain H-4/L-2/E spouses with unexpired I-94) from 180 to up to 540 days, retroactive to renewals filed on/after 2022-05-04. STILL GOVERNS renewal applications filed BEFORE 2025-10-30; superseded for renewal applications filed on or after that date. Renamed from ead-auto-extension-540-days to avoid rule_id collision with rules_greencard.json.

rules.source_citation: DHS interim final rule, Removal of the Automatic Extension of Employment Authorization Documents, 90 FR 48799 (Oct. 30, 2025), Doc. No. 2025-19702
rules.notes: No automatic extension of employment authorization/EADs for Form I-765 RENEWAL applications filed on or after 2025-10-30; renewals filed before that date keep their up-to-540-day extension. Exceptions preserved: TPS-related extensions established by statute or specific Federal Register notices, and the F-1 STEM OPT 180-day extension. Litigation as of 2026-07-06: Doe v. DHS, No. 8:26-cv-00060 (C.D. Cal., filed 2026-01-08 by H-4 dependent spouses) remains PENDING - TRO application converted to a preliminary-injunction motion 2026-05-08, hearing held 2026-06-24 before Judge David O. Carter, no substantive ruling issued as of 2026-07-06; a second suit, Doe v. USCIS, No. 1:26-cv-01336 (D.D.C., filed 2026-04-20 by a VAWA-based EAD holder), was voluntarily dismissed 2026-06-02. No injunction or vacatur - rule IN FORCE as of 2026-07-06, but monitor the pending C.D. Cal. PI motion. Employers must track EAD expirations strictly for renewals filed on/after 2025-10-30 (affects i485_pending c9, h1b spouses/H-4, and similar categories). Renamed from ead-auto-extension-removed-2025 to avoid rule_id collision with rules_greencard.json.

F-1 student whose post-completion OPT EAD expires while a timely filed STEM OPT extension application is pending gets an automatic 180-day extension of employment authorization. Expressly unaffected by the 2025-10-30 IFR removing 540-day renewal extensions. For I-9: expired EAD + endorsed I-20 recommending STEM extension documents the extension.

rules.source_citation: E-Verify User Manual 2.2; E-Verify MOU art. II.C
rules.notes: Create a case for each newly hired employee no later than the third business day after the employee's first day of employment for pay. Effective date reflects the longstanding MOU standard (date approximate; standard predates 2013 user manual revisions).

rules.value: After the individual accepts an offer of employment AND Form I-9 is complete; a case may be created before the first day of work for pay, but E-Verify may never be used to pre-screen applicants and employment may not be conditioned on 'passing' E-Verify
rules.source_citation: E-Verify FAQ; E-Verify MOU art. II.C
rules.notes: Pre-employment screening via E-Verify is prohibited by the MOU.

rules.source_citation: E-Verify FAQ; E-Verify User Manual 2.2
rules.notes: If a case is created later than the third business day after the first day of work for pay, E-Verify requires the employer to select or type a reason for the delay; employers who missed the deadline should create the case immediately to come into compliance.

rules.source_citation: E-Verify, Tentative Nonconfirmations (Mismatches); E-Verify User Manual 3.3
rules.notes: After a TNC/mismatch is issued, the employer must promptly notify the employee privately and complete notification/referral steps within 10 federal government working days; the employee then has 10 federal government working days from issuance to tell the employer whether they will take action. If no decision by day 10, employer should close the case. Effective date approximate (current 'mismatch' process terminology adopted 2021). Unit is federal government working days (typed business_days so the validator does not misread it as calendar days).

rules.source_citation: E-Verify, How to Process a Tentative Nonconfirmation (Mismatch)
rules.notes: After referral, the employee has 8 federal government working days to visit an SSA field office or contact DHS to begin resolving the mismatch. Longstanding standard; effective date approximate. Unit is federal government working days (typed business_days so the validator does not misread it as calendar days).

rules.source_citation: E-Verify MOU art. II.C; E-Verify Mismatch guidance
rules.notes: Employer may not terminate, suspend, delay training, withhold or lower pay, or otherwise take adverse action against an employee because of a mismatch while the case is interim/pending. Employment may be terminated only after a Final Nonconfirmation.

rules.value: U.S. Passport or Passport Card, Permanent Resident Card (Form I-551), and Employment Authorization Document (Form I-766): when presented for Form I-9, E-Verify displays the government photo for comparison and the employer MUST retain a copy of the document presented
rules.source_citation: E-Verify User Manual 2.3 (photo matching); E-Verify MOU art. II.C
rules.notes: Photo matching (passports added 2010) compares the E-Verify-displayed photo to the document photo, not to the employee. Effective date approximate for passport photo matching rollout.

Contractor not already enrolled must enroll as a Federal Contractor in E-Verify within 30 calendar days of contract award. Within 90 calendar days of enrollment, begin initiating verification of all new hires within 3 business days after their date of hire. Clause applies to qualifying federal contracts above the simplified acquisition threshold with US performance and 120+ day period.

rules.value: For each existing employee assigned to the covered contract, initiate an E-Verify case within 90 calendar days after enrollment as a Federal Contractor OR within 30 calendar days of the employee's assignment to the contract, whichever is later
rules.notes: Federal contractors are the only employers permitted (and required) to run E-Verify on certain existing employees.

rules.source_citation: E-Verify MOU art. II.C
rules.notes: Enrolled employers must display the E-Verify Participation poster and the IER Right to Work poster (English and Spanish) where hiring occurs, visible to prospective employees.

rules.source_citation: 8 U.S.C. 1324b(a)(2); 28 CFR Part 44
rules.notes: Employers with 4 or more employees may not discriminate in hiring, firing, or recruitment/referral for a fee based on citizenship status (protected classes include U.S. citizens, U.S. nationals, asylees, refugees, and recent LPRs). National origin discrimination is covered by 1324b for employers with 4-14 employees; Title VII covers 15+. Enforced by DOJ IER.

During I-9/E-Verify, requesting more or different documents than required, requesting specific documents, or rejecting facially genuine documents, if done for purposes or with the intent of discriminating based on citizenship status or national origin, is an unfair immigration-related employment practice. Added by Immigration Act of 1990; IIRIRA (1996) added the intent requirement.

rules.value: 288-2861 per Form I-9
rules.source_citation: 8 CFR 274a.10(b)(2); DHS Civil Monetary Penalty Adjustments for Inflation (Jan. 2, 2025), Doc. No. 2024-31204
rules.notes: Substantive/uncorrected technical Form I-9 violations. These 2025-adjusted amounts REMAIN CURRENT as of 2026-07-06 because the 2026 annual inflation adjustment was cancelled (see penalty-2026-inflation-adjustment-cancelled).

rules.value: First order: 716-5,724 per unauthorized worker; second order: 5,724-14,308; third or subsequent order: 8,586-28,619
rules.source_citation: 8 CFR 274a.10(b)(1); DHS Civil Monetary Penalty Adjustments for Inflation (Jan. 2, 2025)
rules.notes: Civil penalties for knowingly hiring or knowingly continuing to employ unauthorized workers, per worker, as adjusted effective 2025-01-02 and still current as of 2026-07-06 (2026 adjustment cancelled per OMB M-26-11). All three tiers ($716-$5,724; $5,724-$14,308; $8,586-$28,619) confirmed against the Federal Register full text of the Jan. 2, 2025 DHS adjustment, Doc. No. 2024-31204, Table 2.

rules.value: Cancelled - no 2026 inflation adjustment; January 2, 2025 amounts remain in effect
rules.source_citation: OMB Memorandum M-26-11 (Apr. 17, 2026); cited in, e.g., No Adjustment of Civil Penalties for Inflation, 91 FR (May 12, 2026), Doc. No. 2026-09334
rules.notes: Because BLS did not publish the October 2025 CPI-U (lapse in appropriations) and the Inflation Adjustment Act permits no alternative index, OMB directed agencies to cancel 2026 penalty inflation adjustments; DHS published no 2026 adjustment for INA 274A penalties. Issuance date confirmed via official Federal Register notice (Doc. No. 2026-09334): 'On April 17, 2026, OMB issued memorandum M-26-11 informing agencies of the cancelation of the inflation adjustment for 2026.' Agencies continue using 2025 CMP levels until the next required adjustment (due by Jan. 15, 2027).

rules.value: ICE's March 16, 2026 Form I-9 Inspection fact sheet superseded the 1997 Virtue Memorandum: more than 10 error categories previously treated as correctable technical violations (e.g., missing date of birth, missing A-number/USCIS number, missing signature dates, incomplete Section 2 document data even when copies are retained, remote-verification procedure failures, electronic I-9 system deficiencies) are reclassified as substantive violations subject to immediate fines
rules.source_citation: ICE Fact Sheet, Form I-9 Inspection Under INA 274A (Mar. 2026)
rules.notes: UNVERIFIED: the primary ICE fact sheet text could not be retrieved (ice.gov blocks automated access); the 2026-03-16 date and the technical-to-substantive reclassification (including Section 2 document-data errors regardless of retained copies, remote/alternative-procedure failures, and electronic I-9 system deficiencies) are corroborated by multiple independent secondary sources (Morgan Lewis, Holland & Knight, Littler, Greenberg Traurig, National Law Review, CUPA-HR alerts, Apr.-Jun. 2026), all consistent. Issued without Federal Register notice or rulemaking. Materially raises audit exposure; substantive violations are not curable and carry the $288-$2,861 per-form penalty range.

Employers get at least 10 business days after notice to correct technical or procedural verification failures made in good faith. Still available as of 2026-07-06, but ONLY for the narrowed set of errors ICE's March 2026 fact sheet still classifies as technical (e.g., missing SSN in Section 1, incomplete name fields); see ice-i9-inspection-policy-2026.

rules.value: A Form I-94/I-94A issued to a work-authorized nonimmigrant because of their status (e.g., H-1B) can serve as a List C item 7 employment-authorization document; the employee must also present a List B identity document; for employer-specific workers the more common path is the List A foreign passport + I-94 combination
rules.source_citation: 8 CFR 274a.2(b)(1)(v)(C)(7); USCIS Lists of Acceptable Documents, List C item 7
rules.notes: Also applies to l1, o1, tn, e3 and other status-based work-authorized nonimmigrants; H-1B portability workers may work for the new employer upon USCIS receipt of the new petition (h1b_transfer_pending) and document I-9 with the current I-94 plus receipt notice per M-274 section 6.4.

rules.value: A Form I-9 is required for every individual hired for employment in the United States after November 6, 1986, including citizens and noncitizens; independent contractors and certain casual domestic workers are excluded; employees hired on or before 11/6/1986 who have been continuously employed are grandfathered
rules.source_citation: INA 274A; 8 CFR 274a.2(b); 8 CFR 274a.7; USCIS M-274 section 2.0
rules.notes: Employers may not use contract structures knowingly to obtain the labor of unauthorized workers (8 CFR 274a.5).
